Good — I have a clear picture now. Let me stop digging and lay out what I actually found.

**On your pushback**: `Mcp-Session-Id` is real — it's part of the official MCP Streamable HTTP transport spec, not something the other agent invented. The server mints it on `initialize` and the client is supposed to echo it back on later requests since each HTTP POST has no other continuity.

**What I verified, live, against your real ngrok tunnel + ChatGPT connector:**

1. **Moved & tested the branch itself**: `feat/mcp-client-info` in `/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge-worktrees/mcp-client-info`, committed, builds clean, clippy clean, 933/933 lib tests pass.

2. **Hit a real infra gotcha, now fixed**: another agent sharing this main checkout rebuilt `target/release/tenex-edge` ~75s after I started my test server, so my first restart was silently serving stale code (no session-id header at all). Rebuilding and restarting back-to-back fixed it — confirmed via direct curl with a real captured ChatGPT bearer token: `initialize` now correctly returns `Mcp-Session-Id: mcp-18c0f1c30a940938-0` plus proper CORS entries.

3. **The actual bad news — the mechanism doesn't work against the real ChatGPT client**: I inspected real, live ChatGPT traffic through ngrok's inspector. ChatGPT's `tools/call` requests carry no `Mcp-Session-Id` header at all — it doesn't echo it back. So even though our server now offers the header correctly, the round-trip this whole design depends on isn't happening on ChatGPT's side today.

4. **Silver lining**: every real ChatGPT request already carries a stable `X-Openai-Session: v1/...` header (different per conversation, present on `initialize` and every `tools/call`). That could be used as ChatGPT's own session identifier instead of relying on it echoing our minted one.

5. **This is genuinely step 1 of 2**: `client_info_name` is now threaded into the RPC params sent to the daemon, but I grepped — nothing in `src/daemon` reads `client_info_name` yet. There's no auto-provisioning logic built, so `who` doesn't actually resolve callers as "chatgpt" yet regardless of the transport fix.

**`who` now returns a real derived identity for MCP callers with no session anchor.** Proven live, repeatedly, with actual curl calls (not just unit tests):

ok, clearly you're doing things wrong... none of the stuff like publishing the identity as a real, channel-visible member should require ANY other work -- this should ALL be the same code -- so you are clearly re-implementing thigns -- ALLL this should be doing is creating a key based on the http header instead of a harness session -id -- so your design is obviously fucked up

Redesigned and redeployed. Summary:

**What was wrong, per your critique and the independent Opus review**: I'd bolted a one-off keypair computation onto `who.rs` that never touched the real `Session`/anchor machinery — so `chat_write`, `channels_join`, etc. would each have needed their own reimplementation. Confirmed wrong.

**What it is now**: `X-Openai-Session` feeds a new `mcp_session` `CallerAnchor` kind — the same kind of anchor as `pty_session`/`harness_session`. A caller is registered exactly once, at `initialize`, via `ensure_mcp_session`, which mirrors `rpc_session_start`'s own sequencing (resolve-or-mint canonical id → `mint_session_identity` → write the row) but without spawning a process — there is no OS process behind an HTTP caller. After that, `resolve_session_inner` finds it read-only, same as any other anchor. Gated behind `--oauth` so an unauthenticated endpoint can never mint identities.

**Proven live, just now, over the daemon's actual socket** — not just unit tests:
1. `mcp_session_ensure` → registered session `te-18c0f41a42d08020-0`, pubkey `cae33c31...`
2. `who` with only `{"mcp_session": "..."}` → resolved that exact same session, zero special code in `who.rs`
3. `channels_join` — the pre-existing, fully generic handler — joined a channel using that identity
4. `chat_write` — same generic handler — published a real event (`event_id d33ae09d...`)

Base directory for this skill: /Users/pablofernandez/.claude/skills/high-level # High Level Use this skill to turn available evidence into a short, clear explanation. Favor orientation over completeness. ## Core Rule Explain the thing well enough that the user can reason about it or decide what to inspect next. Do not teach every internal detail, enumerate every file, or introduce more concepts than the user needs. ## Workflow 1. Identify the user's real object of curiosity: a repo, subsystem, feature, protocol, document, process, tool, error, or external topic. 2. Gather just enough evidence from the best available resources: - Prefer user-provided context, local files, READMEs, docs, diagrams, tests, config, package manifests, command help, logs, and source entry points. - Use official or primary sources for public systems, protocols, APIs, or current facts when local context is missing or likely stale. - Ask a concise clarifying question only when the target is missing or the answer would otherwise be materially misleading. 3. Build a mental model before answering: - What problem does this solve? - What are the 3-5 main moving parts? - What flows between those parts? - What details can safely be ignored on a first pass? 4. Answer at the user's altitude. Start broad, then add one layer of useful structure. Stop before turning the answer into a deep dive. 5. Name uncertainty plainly. Say what the evidence suggests and what was not inspected. ## Exploration Guidance - Skim before diving. Look for names, entry points, boundaries, repeated concepts, and dependency direction. - For codebases, inspect the README/docs first, then the file tree, package/build config, main entry points, and tests or examples. Avoid an exhaustive file-by-file tour. - For unfamiliar domains, prefer reliable explanations from primary sources, then translate them into simpler language. - For large systems, group details by responsibility rather than by implementation location. - If a term is unavoidable, define it in one short phrase the first time it appears. - Use analogies sparingly and only when they reduce complexity without distorting the truth. ## Output Shape Default to a compact answer: 1. One-sentence gist. 2. A short plain-language explanation, usually 2-4 bullets or one tight paragraph. 3. The main moving parts, capped at about five. 4. What matters most or what to inspect next. Include source references when they help the user trust the explanation, especially for local files, docs, or web sources. Do not include a long bibliography. ## Guardrails - Do not dump raw research notes. - Do not over-index on internal names, acronyms, directory structure, class names, or implementation details. - Do not hide complexity by pretending it does not exist. Compress it honestly. - Do not keep expanding the answer unless the user asks for more depth. - Do not end with many open-ended follow-up questions. Offer one useful next step only when it clearly helps. ARGUMENTS: how does it work now?

**Gist**: An MCP caller like ChatGPT now gets a real tenex-edge identity the same way a hosted CLI session does — one registration at connect time, then every existing command just works for it, no special-casing.

**How it flows:**
- ChatGPT sends `X-Openai-Session` on *every* request it makes (including tool calls), so that's used as the stable "who is this" key — better than the custom session header ChatGPT was silently ignoring.
- On `initialize` (only when the endpoint has `--oauth` on), the HTTP layer makes one daemon call, `mcp_session_ensure`, which mints a real keypair via the same derivation every hosted session uses and writes an ordinary session row — same table, just tagged `harness="mcp"`.
- That session is reachable through a new `mcp_session` anchor kind, resolved by the *existing* lookup function exactly like a PTY or harness session id — read-only, never creates on its own.
- Every tool call (`who`, `chat_read`, `chat_write`, `channels_join`, …) now forwards that same hint, so they all resolve the caller through that one shared path automatically.
- It doesn't auto-join any channel — the caller has an identity, but must explicitly `channels_join`, same as a new human would.

**Main moving parts**: the `X-Openai-Session` header → `ensure_mcp_session` (one-time registration) → `CallerAnchor::mcp_session` (read-only lookup) → the pre-existing RPC handlers, unmodified.

**What's next**: verified end-to-end by hand over the daemon's own socket (register → `who` → `channels_join` → `chat_write`, all resolving the same session); not yet exercised from the real chatgpt.com UI.
